text: I usually have something simple yet nourishing for breakfast. A cup of tea, perhaps some toast with butter and jam, maybe some scrambled eggs or an omelette if I'm feeling peckish. If my grandkids come round, we might even make some pancakes together, which always brings a bit of joy into our morning. Nothing too fancy, just a hearty start to the day.

My typical weekday lunches are quite simple really. Since my son still lives nearby, he usually picks me up on his way home from work and we have a spot of tea together before settling in for some cake and a chat. If it's a busy morning, I might make something myself – perhaps a sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner. Sometimes, though, our grandkids will come round and join us too; they love having Grandma cook them a treat! We'll probably spend the afternoon playing games or watching a film while enjoying each other's company. On Fridays, however, my daughter usually drops by and we share a meal together before catching up on her latest projects. All in all, it's lovely to unwind with loved ones in the middle of the day.
I tend to be in bed by 10 pm most nights, getting a bit of rest before waking up again at 6:30 am. It's helped me manage my daily routines quite effectively, even now that I'm semi-retired.

I think my approach to daily self-care is quite simple, really. It's all about finding balance and making sure I'm taking care of myself so I can be there for others. For me, getting a good night's sleep is essential – nothing like winding down before bed with a cuppa and some quiet time to yourself. A warm bath and a bit of pampering every now and again helps too! In terms of exercise, I try to fit in a brisk walk during the day whenever I can, maybe catch up with some stretching afterwards. And of course, spending time tending to my garden brings me such joy – watching those little seeds sprout into something beautiful is just wonderful! It's funny how much more energised and focused I feel once I've taken a break and done something creative, whether it's writing in my journal, working on a puzzle, or even just doodling in my notebook. Giving myself permission to take breaks and recharge throughout the day makes all the difference.

I try to be a bit informal while still being professional. You know, make connections rather than pushing people away. In my previous roles, networking was more of an exchange – sharing advice, asking for guidance, lending a hand where needed. I'd attend department meetings, contribute to team discussions, offer support during busy periods... that sort of thing. It feels like building relationships really helps me understand what others need, which is essential for getting things done effectively. Plus, word-of-mouth has always been a powerful tool, so I encourage colleagues to speak positively about our work whenever possible.

I find a regular routine helps me stay focused while working from home. I like starting my day quite early, usually around 7 am. After getting some exercise – perhaps going for a brisk walk or doing a bit of yoga – I make myself a cuppa tea and sit down at my desk with my trusty laptop and a warm blanket. It sets the tone for a productive morning. Once settled, I take out my planner and break down my tasks into smaller chunks, prioritising what needs to be done first. Having everything laid out makes it easier to tackle each task without feeling overwhelmed. Of course, having a tidy workspace is essential; I try to keep clutter at bay so nothing distracts me mid-task. My cat even likes sitting nearby, which always provides an entertaining companion! Throughout the day, taking breaks to stretch, grab another cuppa, and step outside for some fresh air really does wonders for keeping me refreshed and ready for whatever comes next.

I'm getting more used to them now, thank goodness! My grandkids have helped me set everything up, so it's a bit easier. We'll be chatting away on my iPad, and sometimes we even play games together – that always brightens up the day. When I need someone else to talk to, like our daughter who lives far away, I'll call her on my phone. It's nice to catch up, but I still prefer talking face-to-face whenever possible. Video calls can feel a bit awkward at first, but once everyone settles in, it works out alright. Now, let me tell you all about what happened during our last one...

You know, I think my love of routine has helped me quite a bit since retiring. Even though I'm not having an official workday anymore, I still try to stick to some sort of daily schedule. Mornings are always my favourite – getting up early gives me plenty of time for a cuppa, some reading, maybe even a spot of gardening before breakfast. It sets the tone for the rest of the day, really helps me feel productive. And, of course, there's always something to be done around the house, whether it's tidying up, cooking dinner, or organising my grandkids' schedules. Keeping busy like that makes the days go by nicely and prevents any feelings of boredom or aimlessness. Plus, staying connected with family is essential for me. Regular phone calls, video chats, and visits make sure I stay linked with them, which keeps me feeling engaged and purposeful. And, let's be honest, keeping myself occupied also stops me worrying too much about what I used to do every day! So, while it might take some effort to adjust, I find that sticking to routines and finding activities that bring me joy have been key to staying motivated in retirement so far.

To be honest, I've always been someone who likes a bit of structure and planning ahead, so adjusting to changes can take some getting used to! When there's something new on the horizon, I like to try to anticipate how it might affect my workload and adjust accordingly. But sometimes those plans can go awry, and that's where flexibility comes in. I think the key is staying focused on what really matters – meeting deadlines and delivering results. If someone gives me a revised priority list, I'll usually re-prioritize based on urgency and importance. It helps to have an open conversation with them to make sure we're aligned and clarify any doubts. Sometimes, adapting to a shift in priorities requires a bit more effort upfront, but once I'm adjusted, I find it easier to manage. The big thing is being able to roll with the punches and knowing when to push forward even if circumstances aren't ideal. Of course, I wouldn't say I thrive in chaos, but I can cope and adapt when necessary! It's funny, though – now that I'm fully retired, having nothing much to rush towards except maybe keeping myself busy, I appreciate all sorts of little adjustments in daily routines! A nice cuppa in the morning becomes a bigger deal; simple pleasures become

I'd say I'm quite assertive, once you know me well enough! *laughs* Seriously though, I believe it's essential to be clear and direct when communicating my thoughts and concerns, while still being polite and considerate towards others. In a professional setting, I try to focus on finding solutions rather than placing blame or getting drawn into arguments. When discussing issues or requests, I usually start by explaining how something affects me directly – whether it's workload, deadlines, or resources – and what specific changes I think could improve our processes. If needed, I'll provide examples from previous projects or instances where we achieved better results with certain approaches. If someone disagrees or has differing opinions, I listen attentively to their perspective and respond thoughtfully. Sometimes, a bit of friendly banter or a joke helps break the tension, but ultimately, I strive to find common ground and compromise whenever feasible. As a former admin assistant, I was taught the importance of knowing one's limits and asking for support when required. So, if I feel uncertain or unsure, I wouldn't hesitate to ask colleagues or supervisors for guidance or clarification. After all, there's always more to learn and appreciate different viewpoints! It might sound old-fashioned, but I genuinely value face-to-face conversations over emails

I think I tend to take a deep breath, gather my thoughts, and try to rephrase what I'm trying to say. Sometimes, I'll even ask someone to repeat back what they thought I meant, so we're on the same page. If all else fails, I might explain myself again in different words until I feel like I've made my point clearly enough. It's funny, isn't it, how some people seem to pick up on certain phrases or tone of voice more than others? My late husband used to tease me that I was too much of an enigma – lots of layers beneath! But honestly, I'd rather be understood correctly once than have everyone jumping to conclusions about me.

I love spending time with my grandkids! When we're all together, we usually have a lovely meal, perhaps some games or quizzes afterwards. My son always makes sure there's plenty of food, so everyone has something to eat. The kids are getting older now, so we try to find common ground – they like watching telly shows or playing board games, whereas I'm more into books, but we manage to chat about everything. Sometimes Joan comes along too; her husband is away for work, so we make a day of it. We might go out for a walk or visit someone nearby before dinner. It's wonderful catching up with them all.

Honestly, I'm not really sure why anyone would bother with something like LinkedIn anymore. In my line of work, word-of-mouth referrals were always more valuable, and we didn't need all those extraneous networking tools. Now, I suppose some people might find it helpful to connect with others in their industry, but personally, I think I'd struggle keeping up with it all. Too much noise and not enough substance. Give me a cuppa and a chat face-to-face any day!

I'd say I'm quite honest and direct in my conversations, which sometimes gets me into trouble, but I believe it's always better to be upfront and clear rather than beating around the bush. However, I try to consider who I'm speaking to and tailor my tone accordingly. With close friends and family, I know we're all adults and can handle a bit of straight talking; but with people I don't know too well, like colleagues or acquaintances, I'll soften the blow slightly to avoid offending them unnecessarily. My late husband used to tease me about being a "straight shooter," so I suppose he was right! Overall, though, I think open and honest communication is essential – it saves misunderstandings in the long run.

I'd say I'm quite level-headed and like to think before acting on impulse. If someone tries to persuade me into something I'm not entirely convinced is a good idea, I'll usually ask them to explain why they're so keen on it and listen attentively to what they have to say. Sometimes, having an open mind helps clarify any doubts I may have had initially. As for actual actions, I tend to stick to my original decision unless there's compelling reason otherwise – be it logic or advice from trusted people close to me. My grandmother used to say, "If your instincts tell you one thing, trust those." So, generally speaking, I try to follow my gut feeling rather than conform solely based on peer opinions.

To be honest, I find those targeted ads a bit annoying. They seem like they're following me everywhere I go! I try to avoid them whenever I can, usually by using my browser's ad blocker or avoiding certain websites altogether. If I need something specific, I'll look it up myself instead of relying on Google to show me what other people might buy. My grandkids always say I'm out of touch because I complain about these sorts of things, but I think it's only fair that everyone has some control over how we're marketed to. Don't know why companies have to follow us all around the internet anyway...

To be honest, I struggle a bit with all these new-fangled gadgets! When I'm browsing the internet, I usually stick to one tab open at a time, unless I need to refer back to something specific. If there are multiple emails or messages I need to sort through, I'll have them in separate tabs, but generally, I prefer keeping my screen clutter-free so I can focus better. My granddaughter has helped me set up some shortcuts, like having my regular websites saved as bookmarks, which makes it easier to find what I need quickly. But overall, simplicity is key for an older mind like mine – too many tabs make my head spin!

To be honest, I find most trolls aren't worth my time. If someone takes the trouble to post a nasty comment, usually because they're feeling lonely themselves, I try to ignore them completely. It makes me wonder what could have driven them to behave like that, so perhaps we all need a bit more kindness towards each other. As for writing responses, I'll sometimes take out a minute to craft a polite reply, explaining how their words affected me and encouraging them to think twice before posting something hurtful again. But generally, I believe ignoring is the best response – there's enough negativity elsewhere in the world; let's focus on spreading positivity instead!

I'm afraid I always seem to struggle a bit with those! If there's an issue during our video call, my first instinct is usually to try and troubleshoot together until we find a solution. Sometimes I'll ask one of my grandsons to lend me his expertise – he's very handy with computers, bless him! If all else fails, I might have to admit defeat and suggest we reschedule for another time. My late husband was quite skilled with technology himself, so sometimes I think he'd be laughing away now at how much more complicated everything has become since his passing... But seriously, I appreciate any assistance I can get from people who know what they're doing!

I make sure to set reminders a fortnight before each bill is due date, so my son checks the calendar and sends me an automated message saying "Payday reminder". If he forgets or misses those messages, our daughter will be checking her mum's bank account, which helps too! Also, every month we review all the upcoming payments together, making any necessary adjustments and discussing what expenses need prioritising first. We stick to having one main spending day – usually Wednesday evenings – where everything else goes into separate jars labeled by category, like groceries, utilities, and entertainment. It allows us to physically touch base with money before putting it away, keeping track of how much's been spent during the week.

I'm rather methodical about my subscriptions. Before cancelling any, I make a list and weigh the benefits against what I actually use them for. If something hasn't been used in ages, like that streaming service we all have too many of these days, out it goes! But if there's still value in having an account – perhaps occasionally watching a show or two – I'll consider keeping it unless costs start adding up unnecessarily. My rule is really quite simple: waste not, want not!

I'm a saver, so I like to think before spending money, even on big-ticket items. Before making any purchase, I'll usually weigh my options, research different models, read reviews, and consider how much I really need something. If I can afford it now, I tend to opt for buying rather than waiting for a sale, unless there's an exceptional offer. My late husband was always telling me, "Maggie, we're not getting any younger," which made me realize that some investments are worth taking out loans for – though I still try to avoid them whenever possible! These days, I'd probably discuss the idea with our son who sometimes helps us sort out such decisions. We take turns choosing what to buy, too, keeping each other accountable for our spending habits.

I'd always say that I'm a bit more inclined towards buying second-hand whenever I can manage. You know, there's something satisfying about finding a great deal and giving an old item a new lease on life. Plus, all those memories people put into making and using these items before me – it's lovely to think that I might be able to bring some joy to someone else too. Of course, sometimes it makes sense to buy brand-new, like if what we need is really urgent or specific features-wise. But generally speaking, my rule of thumb is "if it works and serves its purpose, why spend extra money?"

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned saver, really. My husband used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned." He was quite right, wasn't he? As soon as we stopped paying our mortgage, my first thought was to make sure we had enough set aside to cover any emergencies. We made a point of having three months' worth of expenses stashed away before buying something big like a new appliance or holiday. And since his passing, I've kept sticking to those habits – always looking ahead and making sure we're financially secure. Plus, I believe that living below your means has served us all these years; there's no need for unnecessary spending, if you ask me!

I take advantage of them whenever I can! You'd be surprised how much money we can save during Black Friday and Boxing Day. My son always says I'm a master of waiting until the last minute before making purchases, which isn't entirely untrue – I like to make sure everything is really worth buying, even if it does mean missing out on some initial discounts. Still, there's nothing quite like scoring a bargain for something essential or one my grandkids will appreciate. It's all part of being thrifty, you know!

Portion control is something my mother taught me when I was a child, really reinforced during my wedding reception buffet days! You know, everything has its place and moderation is key. When cooking for myself now, I make sure to measure out ingredients so there isn't too much waste. And when eating out, we usually stick to smaller portions – helps avoid those dreaded food comas afterwards. It's all about balance and enjoying what you eat rather than devouring everything in sight. My late husband always said, "A little bit of pleasure never hurt anyone." Still live by those words today.

I think a gentle and honest approach is best for me. Someone who will listen attentively, ask open-ended questions, and avoid being too confrontational or prescriptive. It's all about exploring my thoughts and feelings in a safe space where I feel heard and understood. A bit like talking to an old friend, really – we'd chat about what's been going on and work together to find some solutions. If someone could meet halfway between traditional talk therapy and a friendly conversation, that would be perfect!

Preventing burnout is really key for me, now more so since retiring. For a while there, I thought retirement was all relaxation, but I soon realised how much rest and downtime were necessary to avoid exhaustion. To be honest, my morning person nature helps – getting up early gives me some peace before the world wakes up. Taking regular breaks during the day isn’t too difficult; usually, I'd take a walk outside or sit quietly in our little garden, collect myself. Doing something creative like knitting or cooking makes me feel refreshed. Of course, keeping busy is essential – whether that’s tending to plants, joining the local allotment committee or writing down family history – having an agenda always keeps mind occupied. For work-related stuff, setting priorities has been crucial. Knowing exactly what must be done each day minimises worries about being swamped. And let’s not forget exercise! Getting out into fresh air, even if it’s just a brisk walk, does wonders for clearing my thoughts and boosting spirits. I believe taking it easy when needed is vital too. Sometimes saying "no" to social invitations simply because one feels fatigued lets others know it's okay to set limits oneself. Having grandkids who still live close-by actually encourages staying grounded, knowing

I find that establishing a relaxing bedtime routine helps me wind down before bed. It could be something like having a warm bath, sipping herbal tea, or simply sitting quietly with a book. Then, getting into bed at a reasonable hour is crucial – for me, it has to be quite early! A darkened room, cool temperature, and minimal noise all contribute to a restful night's sleep. Of course, sticking to my usual daily routine during the weekdays, too, seems to make it easier to settle in at nights.

I try to mix it up a bit each week, really. During the summer months, there's nothing better than getting out into my garden to tend to the vegetables and flowers. There's something therapeutic about being outdoors, feeling the sun on my face and watching everything come alive. My grandkids love helping me water the plants too! In the colder winter months, I might take shorter walks round the block, which helps keep me warm. If I'm feeling energetic enough, we'll go for longer strolls along the canal towpath nearby. The fresh air does wonders for clearing my mind. On occasion, I'll treat myself to a gentle yoga class at our local community centre – always nice to stretch those creaky joints! Occasionally, I join some of the other ladies from the village for a brisk walk or bike ride. It's lovely catching up while keeping fit. Nothing like having someone to chat with, making the miles disappear before you know it. Of course, I have to be mindful of my energy levels; two o'clock tends to be my post-lunch slump, so I usually pace myself accordingly. But overall, varying my routine keeps things interesting and ensures I remain active throughout the year.

I think I weigh my options fairly systematically, considering what will work best for me personally and practically. When faced with choices, I tend to break them down into pros and cons, looking at how each option might impact various aspects of my life – finances, relationships, personal comfort, etc. Sometimes, I'll even make a little list, although I'm not too fussy about keeping track; it's more about having something to refer back to later. Once I have all the facts laid out, I try to imagine myself making the decision, using my intuition to guide me towards the choice that feels most sensible and enjoyable. Of course, there may be moments where rational thought is clouded by emotion – which usually happens during those pesky times when grandchildren come to visit! In such cases, I remind myself to take a step back, breathe deeply, and trust my initial gut feeling. It's always helpful to talk through my thoughts with someone close like a trusted child or partner, though sometimes I find it's better to go with my first instinct rather than waiting for reassurance. And ultimately, I believe it comes down to being honest with myself – what really makes sense, and what's truly best for me. As an old saying goes: "patience and curiosity get

I think incorporating feedback is a vital part of any process, whether personal or professional. When receiving feedback, I try to listen attentively and consider all aspects of what was said. It helps me gain a better understanding of my actions and how they impact others. Once I have taken everything in, I'll weigh up the pros and cons of different options before making an informed decision. Sometimes, I might need some time to reflect on the information given, but ultimately, I believe open-mindedness allows me to implement changes effectively. By being receptive to constructive criticisms and using them constructively, we can refine our approaches and improve overall outcomes.

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite methodical. When faced with a complex issue or problem, I like to break it down into smaller parts, consider each aspect separately, and evaluate them systematically. It's almost like taking a step-by-step journey, where I gather information, assess the facts, weigh the pros and cons, and make informed decisions based on logic rather than intuition. Sometimes, having some time to reflect before making a decision helps me clarify my thoughts and arrive at a more considered conclusion. Of course, there have been instances where jumping straight into action has served me better, too – perhaps when every moment counts! But overall, I believe being systematic and deliberate allows me to be a fairer thinker. As I always say, "patience and curiosity" tend to serve us best in getting to the heart of any matter.

I like to read things twice before getting too involved, first a quick glance to make sure I haven't missed any main points, and then another more leisurely go-through to really absorb what I'm reading. Sometimes writing out key phrases or sentences helps me remember them better later on, although I find myself having to look these notes up again because my memory isn't always what it used to be! When faced with complex information, I try breaking it down into smaller chunks and relating it to something I already know – that usually makes sense of it all a bit better. And sometimes talking it through with someone else does wonders; my son is very patient with me, so he'll listen while I ramble away about whatever's got me puzzled!

I think I tend to focus more on what works rather than dwelling too much on what could be improved upon. But if I'm being completely honest, I suppose one area where I'd like to work a bit is probably my digital literacy. As someone who's had limited exposure to cutting-edge tech, I find myself getting frustrated with all these modern tools and platforms. My son has been patiently teaching me how to navigate some of them, which helps, but sometimes I wish there were more straightforward guides available, so we wouldn't have to go round in circles explaining everything multiple times. Still, he says it'll come with practice!

I think my approach is quite simple really. You know, when faced with a new situation, I like to take a step back, breathe, and try to work out why it might be an opportunity rather than something daunting. Sometimes, people tell me to "just go for it" or "be more spontaneous," but I find that doesn't always sit comfortably with me. What works better is taking some time to consider what's involved, weighing the pros and cons, and figuring out how I can make it work for myself. If someone explains things clearly and shows me exactly what's expected, I'm much more likely to feel ready to give it a go. And even if it feels uncomfortable at first, I remind myself that I'll gain experience and confidence along the way. It's all about finding that balance between being open to new experiences and feeling secure enough to stick with them until I get the hang of it. As my mum used to say, "patience and curiosity get you further than rushing through life ever did." I've found that to be true!

I make sure to prioritize my free time, which is a bit precious now that I'm retired. I try to set aside some time each morning before the world gets too busy again. Usually, I'll have a cuppa and sit down with a good book – perhaps something historical fiction or a biography. It helps me start the day feeling relaxed and focused. And, of course, having a warm and cozy spot to curl up in makes all the difference!

I usually find out about new authors or genres through word-of-mouth, like a recommendation from a friend or neighbour who shares my tastes. We'll be chatting while having our morning tea, and someone will say, "Oh, have you read so-and-so?" And we'll start talking about it, and before you know it, we're swapping books and making plans to meet up soon. Sometimes, too, I'll stumble upon an author or genre while browsing in a second-hand shop or library – there's something about flipping through pages of unfamiliar titles that always piques my interest! Occasionally, I might pick up a magazine or a newspaper with reviews or features on different writers or topics, which helps me broaden my horizons even more. But generally speaking, I think human connections and conversations are what lead me to discovering new favourite authors and genres.

I like a nice cuppa and some gentle tunes while I'm getting ready in the morning, maybe tidying up my room before starting my daily routine. It helps me feel more awake and sets a pleasant tone for the day ahead. Sometimes I might even find myself humming along to the lyrics – though I must admit, I rarely remember what they're singing! But there's something soothing about having background noise that makes everything feel more manageable, you know?

I find that podcasting is a bit too much for me, to be honest. All these voices going on, competing for my attention... I mean, I'll occasionally listen to something interesting while I'm out and about, like a documentary-style programme, but only if I know what it's about beforehand. And even then, I tend to prefer being able to pause and rewind whenever needed. As for audiobooks, though, I actually rather enjoy them! They're great for commuting or doing chores. There's something soothing about having someone else read aloud, isn't there? I go quite far with my subscription service – I try to finish one every couple of months or so. My latest favourite was a biography about Florence Nightingale; fascinating stuff! What about you, do you have any favourites?

I thoroughly enjoy tending to my little garden out there! It brings me so much joy watching everything grow and flourish. There's something peaceful about getting my hands dirty and seeing those seeds sprout into beautiful flowers and delicious vegetables. Plus, it's a great way to spend time outdoors, even though I'm not as energetic as I used to be. My grandkids love coming to visit and we have lovely chats while we're planting or harvesting together. Sometimes I'll take them on little nature walks along the nearby woods – we always find fascinating things like berries or interesting insects. It really is wonderful having such wonderful young minds to share these simple pleasures with!

I rather like having some time to myself now and again. It helps me unwind and recharge, especially after a busy morning spent tending to my garden or dealing with paperwork. As someone who enjoys getting out into nature, I find being surrounded by trees and plants calming. Even just sitting quietly in my armchair, reading a book or watching one of those lovely nature documentaries on telly is something I look forward to. It gives me a chance to collect my thoughts and reflect on what's been going on in my life. Of course, since becoming widowed, there have been moments when loneliness crept in, but spending time alone has always helped me come to terms with my circumstances.

I love exploring different flavours! When trying a new cuisine, I usually start by looking at cookbooks or watching cooking programmes to get an idea of what dishes I might like. Then, I'll try making something simple first, maybe a stir-fry or pasta dish, to gauge how much work is involved and whether the ingredients feel too exotic. If everything seems manageable, I'll go out and buy some fresh ingredients – though my grandkids sometimes tease me about being adventurous! If all goes well, we'll have dinner together as a little family experiment. Sometimes, one of them will surprise us with a favourite restaurant find, which has been a great way to introduce new tastes. And once upon a time... I even tried sushi-making with my late husband; although he wasn't keen on raw fish, I was quite taken with the process. Even now, I still occasionally dabble in experimenting with international spices and ingredients when we visit local farmers' markets or food festivals. The most enjoyable part is sharing these discoveries with loved ones and seeing everyone enjoying each other's company while tasting our culinary explorations.

I think a mix is essential, isn't it? You know, too much repetition can be dull, even if it's something we love. For me, my main hobby is gardening – I have a lovely vegetable patch out the back where I grow all sorts of fruits and veggies for our meals. But sometimes, I like to break that up with some longer trips away, like last summer we went on a coach trip to the Lake District. We spent two whole days exploring the lakes and mountains, which was just wonderful. It was nice to take a breather and spend time together as a little group. Then there are those quieter moments, like getting lost in a good book. I'm rather fond of history and biography novels, so I'll put myself out to read one every now and again while watching the world go by outside. And, of course, I still make an effort to bake treats for the grandkids; they always seem to appreciate that. So yeah, having different types of activities helps keep life interesting, don't you agree?

I'm quite particular about keeping a little notebook where I jot down my plans and any notable happenings during the weekend. It helps me remember what we're going out to do, like who's coming over for dinner or which local event we'll be attending. My grandkids always find it amusing how much I scribble away – all those crossings-out and additions! But it keeps everything straight and makes me feel more organised. Occasionally, I might even add some sketches of the gardens or notes about the books I've read so far; that way, I have something tangible to refer to later. Nothing too fancy, mind you, just simple and easy to glance at.

I'm quite particular about keeping my space tidy and organized. It started with a bit of habit, really – my late husband was always saying how lovely our little flat used to be. Now, since he passed away, I like to think I carry on his memory by making sure everything has its place. My daily routine helps too; I make a cuppa every morning, sit down with the paper, plan out what needs doing, and tick items off one by one. A simple list is very effective. For more complex tasks, I break them into smaller chunks, maybe even set reminders on my trusty little calendar to keep me on track. My grandkids love getting involved sometimes, we work together to sort out tricky bits or find solutions. It's funny, though, nowadays there's so much digital clutter! Between emails, texts, and notifications, it feels like information is everywhere. Sometimes I wish people could stick to proper post or letters anymore... anyway, I try to avoid checking my device before bed, save all messages until the morning, and only check those once I have some peace and quiet. Simple habits, perhaps, but they seem to suit me just fine! Oh, speaking of which, I almost forgot – my granddaughter asked me recently why I still

I like starting my day a bit later now, so usually around 7:00 am. After getting dressed comfortably in something warm – it might be a favourite scarf or coat, even though we're still quite mild outside – I make myself a cuppa tea and sit down at the kitchen table. While I'm sipping that, I take a moment to review what I have planned for the day ahead, whether it's attending some event, meeting someone, or simply enjoying some me-time in the garden. Sometimes I jot down notes or reminders in my little notebook while having another sip of tea. Once I feel prepared, I'll grab my bag, which is always packed lightly, and set out into the morning. It gives me a chance to breathe the fresh air and listen to the birds singing before the bustle starts. Usually takes me about an hour to get going properly – plenty of time to walk to the local shop, say hello to any neighbours who need chatting...

I try my best to make our little house warm enough during the winter months while keeping costs down. We have a loft insulation check done recently, which helped, and we draught-proofed all doors and windows where necessary. The kids used to complain about how cold it was before that, poor dears! My husband always said I'm like a piggy bank – pinching pennies wherever I can. Anyway, now it feels much cozier indoors. We switch off lights, TVs, and whatnot whenever we're not using them, and turn down the thermostat slightly in the evenings. During summer, I open the curtains during the morning to let sunlight in, so there's no need for electric lighting afterwards. It might sound simple, but these tiny changes add up, don't you think? When grandkids visit, they love staying in my lovely garden room; they find it very pleasant. As an added bonus, the sun shines into there quite nicely most mornings. Oh, and I almost forgot – since retiring, I take pleasure in reorganising cupboards and making sure everything is tidy, including our storage shed outback! A bit of tidying gives me some sense of purpose, I suppose.

I must admit, I'm a bit bewildered by all these "smart" devices everyone seems to be talking about nowadays! To be honest, I find them quite overwhelming. My grandkids try to show me how to use this one thing or another, but honestly, I'd much rather stick to my trusty old kettle and radio – simple pleasures, you know? Nowadays, I only really need something like an Amazon Echo to remind me of upcoming appointments or turn on the lights when I walk into the room, so even that's become more complicated than necessary. But overall, I think we're getting too caught up in having every little device controlling our lives. A bit of human connection is still nice, isn't it?

I think prioritising is quite simple really. You have your must-haves, like staying healthy and having a roof over one's head, and then there are nice-to-haves, such as taking grandkids out or pursuing hobbies. When faced with conflicting demands, I tend to ask myself what will make me most happy in the longer run. Of course, my husband always said I was very thoughtful and considerate, which I try to live up to – making sure everyone's needs are met while still getting some enjoyment ourselves isn't too difficult. Ultimately, though, being realistic helps; we all know we can only do so much!
